title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
There’s a term making the rounds: vibe coding. Coined by Andrej Karpathy, it describes the new mode of AI-assisted development where you describe what you want in natural language, accept the generated code with minimal review, and “forget that the code even exists.”
It’s a catchy label for something real. Tools like Claude Code, GitHub Copilot’s coding agent, and Cursor are genuinely changing how software gets written. But they’ve also quietly removed something that used to matter: the moment when a human read the code and asked, is this actually what I meant?

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
The Problem Isn’t Code Quality
The naive worry about AI-generated code is that it’ll be wrong in obvious ways — bugs, hallucinated APIs, logic errors. And that does happen. But the deeper problem is more subtle.
LLM-generated code is plausible by construction. It compiles. It passes the tests you wrote. It looks like idiomatic Python or Rust or Go. The surface-level quality is often impressive.
What it doesn’t do is know what you meant.
Consider a deceptively simple example: “given a list of integers, remove duplicates.” There are at least two natural interpretations:

Keep one copy of each element that appears more than once: [1,2,3,2,4] → [1,2,3,4]
Remove all elements that appear more than once: [1,2,3,2,4] → [1,3,4]

A typical LLM will produce interpretation 1 without hesitation — because it’s statistically more common in training data. But if you wanted interpretation 2, you now have a bug that looks like working code. It’ll pass most tests you’d think to write. It might ship.
This is the intent gap: the semantic distance between what you meant and what the program does.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
Why Vibe Coding Amplifies It
The intent gap isn’t new. It’s always existed in software engineering. What AI changes is the scale and the invisibility.
Traditional development had natural checkpoints: you wrote the code, you re-read it, a reviewer read it, you debugged it step by step. Each of these was an opportunity to notice that the implementation diverged from the intent.
In the vibe coding workflow, those checkpoints are being bypassed. You type a prompt, accept a diff, and move on. The ratio of code produced to code carefully examined is growing rapidly. AI writes faster than humans can review.
And crucially: AI-generated bugs are harder to spot than hand-written ones, precisely because the code looks right. A human programmer’s mistake often has a “this looks weird” quality. AI-generated misinterpretations are fluent and stylistically impeccable.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
Specifications as the Answer
The standard response to software bugs is testing. Write more tests, better tests, edge case tests. This is good advice but it’s not sufficient here.
Tests check specific inputs. They can’t tell you whether the intent was correctly captured — they can only tell you whether the code matches the tests. And your tests were probably written based on the same informal prompt that generated the code. They’ll share the same blind spots.
What’s needed is something more fundamental: formal specifications. A specification describes what the code should do in a way that can be mechanically checked — not just against the examples you thought to include, but against the space of all possible behaviors.
For the “remove duplicates” example, a formal postcondition makes the intent unambiguous:
# Intent: remove ALL elements that appear more than once
assert all(lst.count(x) == 1 for x in result)
assert all(x in result for x in lst if lst.count(x) == 1)
This is just a Python assertion, but it pins down the semantics precisely. Any implementation that satisfies it is correct; any that doesn’t is wrong. You can check it dynamically, use it for fuzz testing, or feed it to a verifier.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
A Spectrum of Formalism
One reason specifications have historically been unpopular is the perception that they’re all-or-nothing — either you do full formal verification (expensive, requires expertise) or you write tests (cheap, familiar).
But there’s actually a spectrum:

Targeted assertions at points of likely ambiguity — cheap, immediately useful, language-agnostic
Preconditions and postconditions on functions — more systematic, can be checked at runtime
Full functional specs in languages like Dafny or Verus — machine-checkable proofs of correctness
Domain-specific languages from which correct code is synthesized — the spec is the program

The key insight is that even partial specifications at the cheap end of the spectrum give substantial value. You don’t need to prove your whole codebase correct; you just need to pin down the parts where AI is most likely to misinterpret your intent.
Recent work from Microsoft Research (Lahiri et al., FSE 2024) shows that LLM-generated postconditions catch real bugs in production Java codebases — including bugs missed by classical invariant detectors. The same LLMs that create the intent gap can help close it, if you use them to generate specifications rather than just code.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
The Shift That’s Needed
The vibe coding moment has changed the role of the human in software development. We’ve moved from author to supervisor to — in the most extreme cases — passive consumer of AI-generated code.
This isn’t inherently bad. It’s a genuine productivity revolution. But it means the informal review process that used to catch intent gaps is weakened or gone.
The replacement has to be more systematic. Specifications — even lightweight ones — are the scalable mechanism for ensuring that AI-generated code does what you actually meant. Not because formal methods are suddenly easy, but because the alternative (trusting that the LLM guessed correctly) is getting less viable as the stakes get higher.
The question isn’t whether we need specifications. It’s how to make writing them cheap enough that people actually do it.
That’s an open problem worth working on.

Further reading: Lahiri et al., “Can Large Language Models Transform Natural Language Intent into Formal Method Postconditions?”, FSE 2024. The RiSE group at Microsoft Research has a nice blog post covering the broader research agenda.

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
text:
In Wardrop’s model of traffic equilibrium, drivers independently choose routes to minimize their own travel time. Each road’s travel time increases with the number of users on it. The resulting equilibria — where no driver can unilaterally switch routes to save time — are Nash equilibria of a congestion game.
Congestion games are the canonical example of a potential game: a game where all players’ incentives are captured by a single function V, so that any player’s gain from deviating equals the change in V. For traffic, the potential is the sum of individual edge costs integrated over usage. The welfare-maximizing Nash equilibrium minimizes total system travel time — this is Wardrop’s system optimum, and it generally differs from the equilibrium that selfish drivers reach (the gap is the price of anarchy).
Finding the system optimum matters for traffic engineers who want to set tolls or signal timings to nudge selfish behavior toward it. It also matters for understanding how far the selfish equilibrium is from the best achievable outcome.



In evolutionary game theory, the state x \in \Delta_n represents the frequency distribution over n strategies in a large population. Payoffs are frequency-dependent: the fitness of strategy i is \pi_i(x), and strategies with above-average fitness grow. The replicator equation
\dot{x}_i = x_i(\pi_i(x) - \bar\pi(x))
describes how the population evolves. In a potential game with \pi_i(x) = \partial V / \partial x_i, this is the gradient flow of V under the Shahshahani metric on the simplex — the population is climbing the potential landscape.
When the potential has multiple local maxima, different initial conditions lead to different stable equilibria. The global maximum of V is the most fit equilibrium — the one that natural selection would reach if it could coordinate globally. Finding it tells biologists which strategy distributions are evolutionary attractors in the strong sense, and whether the population is likely to be trapped at a suboptimal local equilibrium.



In many economic models, agents choose actions from a finite set and their payoffs depend on the aggregate action distribution. When the game has a potential, the Nash equilibria are exactly the local maxima of V over the simplex. A mechanism designer who can influence payoffs — through taxes, subsidies, or information — wants to know the best achievable Nash equilibrium to target. This requires finding the global maximum of V, not just any local one.

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
text:
In Wardrop’s model of traffic equilibrium, drivers independently choose routes to minimize their own travel time. Each road’s travel time increases with the number of users on it. The resulting equilibria — where no driver can unilaterally switch routes to save time — are Nash equilibria of a congestion game.
Congestion games are the canonical example of a potential game: a game where all players’ incentives are captured by a single function V, so that any player’s gain from deviating equals the change in V. For traffic, the potential is the sum of individual edge costs integrated over usage. The welfare-maximizing Nash equilibrium minimizes total system travel time — this is Wardrop’s system optimum, and it generally differs from the equilibrium that selfish drivers reach (the gap is the price of anarchy).
Finding the system optimum matters for traffic engineers who want to set tolls or signal timings to nudge selfish behavior toward it. It also matters for understanding how far the selfish equilibrium is from the best achievable outcome.



In evolutionary game theory, the state x \in \Delta_n represents the frequency distribution over n strategies in a large population. Payoffs are frequency-dependent: the fitness of strategy i is \pi_i(x), and strategies with above-average fitness grow. The replicator equation
\dot{x}_i = x_i(\pi_i(x) - \bar\pi(x))
describes how the population evolves. In a potential game with \pi_i(x) = \partial V / \partial x_i, this is the gradient flow of V under the Shahshahani metric on the simplex — the population is climbing the potential landscape.
When the potential has multiple local maxima, different initial conditions lead to different stable equilibria. The global maximum of V is the most fit equilibrium — the one that natural selection would reach if it could coordinate globally. Finding it tells biologists which strategy distributions are evolutionary attractors in the strong sense, and whether the population is likely to be trapped at a suboptimal local equilibrium.



In many economic models, agents choose actions from a finite set and their payoffs depend on the aggregate action distribution. When the game has a potential, the Nash equilibria are exactly the local maxima of V over the simplex. A mechanism designer who can influence payoffs — through taxes, subsidies, or information — wants to know the best achievable Nash equilibrium to target. This requires finding the global maximum of V, not just any local one.

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
text:
The Mathematical Structure
All three problems above share the same mathematical core. With n strategies and population state x \in \Delta_n = \{x \geq 0 : \mathbf{1}^\top x = 1\}, the most natural class of potential games has a quadratic potential:
V(x) = x^\top A x + q^\top x
Here A_{ij} captures the interaction between strategies i and j — how much the fitness of strategy i depends on the frequency of j — and q_i is the intrinsic payoff to strategy i, independent of the population state. The gradient \nabla_i V(x) = 2(Ax)_i + q_i is the marginal payoff to strategy i at state x.
Nash equilibria of this game are exactly the KKT points of \max_{x \in \Delta_n} V(x): states where all active strategies have equal (and maximal) payoff, and no inactive strategy has higher payoff. The welfare-maximizing Nash equilibrium is simply the global maximum of V over \Delta_n.
So the problem reduces to:
\max_{x \in \Delta_n}\; x^\top A x + q^\top x \tag{P}
This is a quadratic program on the simplex. When A \preceq 0, the potential V is concave, has a unique global maximum, and (P) is easy — any local solver finds it. The hard and interesting case is when A is indefinite: some eigenvalues positive, some negative. This is generic in practice, since strategic interactions have no reason to be sign-definite.

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
section: Why This Is Hard in General
text:
Why This Is Hard in General

with indefinite A is a nonconvex quadratic program, and nonconvex QPs are NP-hard. But the hardness here is not just a worst-case curiosity — it reflects a real structural difficulty.

Multiple local optima. An indefinite V can have exponentially many local maxima on \Delta_n, each a stable Nash equilibrium. The replicator dynamics and most learning algorithms converge to some local maximum, but which one depends on initialization. The global maximum can be arbitrarily better than a typical local one.
No useful SDP relaxation in general. The standard approach to nonconvex QPs is to relax to a semidefinite program. For this problem the SDP relaxation is:
\max\; \langle \hat{A}, \hat{X} \rangle \quad\text{s.t.}\quad \hat{X} \succeq 0,\; \hat{X}_{00} = 1,\; \text{simplex constraints on } \hat{X}
This is solvable in polynomial time, but the relaxation is generally not tight for indefinite A — the SDP optimum can exceed the true optimum, and rank-1 recovery fails.
Inapproximability. Conitzer and Sandholm (2003) showed that finding a Nash equilibrium maximizing social welfare is NP-hard, and moreover inapproximable in polynomial time: no algorithm can guarantee being within any constant factor of optimal welfare, unless P = NP. This rules out not just exact algorithms but any efficient approximation scheme for the general case.
The picture is bleak. And yet — for a large, natural subclass of games, the problem turns out to be exactly solvable in polynomial time.

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
text:
A Tractable Class via Reparameterization

The key idea
Rather than relaxing (P) to an SDP, we ask a different question: can we find a change of variables x_i = s_i(y_i) — applied componentwise, one coordinate at a time — that makes the objective concave in y?
If such a reparameterization exists, the problem
\max_{y \in \mathcal{S}'}\; V(s(y))
is a concave maximization over a convex set, solvable exactly by any first-order method. The global maximum of V over \Delta_n is then recovered as x^* = s(y^*).


When does it work?
For a componentwise map x_i = s_i(y_i) with s_i smooth and s_i' > 0, the Hessian of V(s(y)) is:
H(y) = \text{diag}(s') \cdot A \cdot \text{diag}(s') + \text{diag}(\nabla V(x) \circ s'')
For concavity we need H(y) \preceq 0, or equivalently (dividing through by \text{diag}(s')^2):
A + \text{diag}(\kappa \circ \nabla V(x)) \preceq 0 \qquad \forall\, x \in \Delta_n
where \kappa_i = s_i'' / (s_i')^2 is the curvature of the reparameterization. For constant curvature \kappa_i = c_i, this becomes a condition on the matrix
M(x) := A + \text{diag}(c \circ \nabla V(x))
which is affine in x. The condition M(x) \preceq 0 for all x \in \Delta_n is therefore a condition on the minimum eigenvalue of an affine matrix pencil — and minimum eigenvalue is concave. A concave function on a simplex attains its minimum at a vertex.


The certification SDP
The simplex \Delta_n has exactly n+1 vertices: v_0 = 0 and v_i = e_i for i = 1, \ldots, n. By the argument above, checking M(x) \preceq 0 everywhere reduces to checking it at these n+1 points. This gives a certification SDP:
\text{find}\; c \geq 0 \;\;\text{s.t.}\;\; A + \text{diag}(c \circ \nabla V(v_k)) \preceq 0, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n \tag{SDP}
with n+1 LMI constraints of size n \times n. If (SDP) is feasible, the reparameterization x_i = -\frac{1}{c_i}\log(1 - c_i y_i) (or x_i = y_i when c_i = 0) concavifies V globally over \Delta_n. We require c \geq 0 to ensure the maps s_i are convex, which keeps the reparameterized domain convex.
The full pipeline is:

Certify: solve (SDP). Polynomial time, n+1 LMIs.
Solve: maximize the reparameterized (now concave) V(s(y)) over the convex y-domain with any gradient method.
Recover: x^* = s(y^*) is the global welfare-maximizing Nash equilibrium.



Why the simplex is special
For a box domain [0,1]^n, the same argument requires checking 2^n vertices — exponential, and useless for large n. The simplex has only n+1 vertices. This is not a coincidence: the simplex is the natural domain for mixed strategies precisely because it has this lean vertex structure. The geometry of the strategy space is doing real work here.

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
section: What Games Are in the Tractable Class?
text:
What Games Are in the Tractable Class?
The feasibility of (SDP) depends on both A and q. At vertex v_0 = 0, the condition becomes A + \text{diag}(c \circ q) \preceq 0. For c \geq 0, this requires q \geq 0 (intrinsic payoffs must be nonnegative) and c_i q_i large enough to dominate the positive eigenvalues of A. At vertex v_i = e_i, the condition involves \nabla V(e_i) = 2Ae_i + q, which mixes the interaction and intrinsic terms.
Intuitively, the SDP is feasible when the intrinsic payoffs q are strong relative to the indefiniteness of A. This is the regime where each strategy has a strong standalone reason to be played, and the strategic interactions are perturbative corrections. In evolutionary terms: external fitness pressures dominate frequency-dependent selection. In economic terms: private values dominate strategic complementarities.
What’s excluded: games where strategic interactions dominate (A strongly indefinite, q small or zero). In particular, homogeneous games (q = 0) are generically outside the tractable class. The Motzkin-Straus problem — finding the maximum clique via \max x^\top A_G x on the simplex with q=0 — is NP-hard, and one can show it is always outside the certifiable class for nontrivial graphs. This is a sanity check: the hardness of max clique is not accidentally circumvented.
The tractable class is convex in (A, q) space — it’s the projection of an SDP feasible set. This means it can be tested efficiently, and it degrades gracefully: as \|q\| grows relative to \|A\|, more and more games become certifiable.

Numerical evidence
On 500 random indefinite quadratic potential games (n = 4, q \sim 3\mathcal{N}(0,I)):




Count
Fraction




Certifiably tractable
411
82%


— with sign-changing gradient (nontrivial)
220
54% of tractable


— with interior global optimum
29
13% of nontrivial


Not certifiable
89
18%



The 29 instances with interior optima are the most significant. The global welfare-maximizing equilibrium is a fully mixed strategy — not achievable by vertex enumeration — and the reparameterization pipeline finds it exactly. All instances were validated against brute-force global search.


The dynamical corollary
In potential games, the replicator dynamics converge to Nash equilibria — but generically to local maxima of V, not the global one. In the certifiable class, after reparameterization, V has a unique global maximum on \Delta_n. This means:






Note



Corollary. For potential games in the certifiable class, the replicator dynamics select the welfare-maximizing Nash equilibrium from almost all initial conditions — and this equilibrium can be computed in polynomial time.


Computational tractability and dynamical stability coincide. Outside the certifiable class, these can come apart: the dynamics may select a suboptimal equilibrium, and finding the optimal one is hard.

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
text:
Open Questions
Characterizing the boundary. The certifiable class is SDP-defined but has no simple closed-form description. Is there a spectral condition on (A, q) — e.g., certifiable if and only if \lambda_{\max}(A) \leq f(\|q\|_\infty, n) for some explicit f — that captures the phase transition?
Beyond constant curvature. Allowing \kappa_i(x_i) = a_i + b_i x_i (affine curvature) makes M(x) quadratic in x, breaking the vertex check. Matrix SOS techniques could certify this on the simplex at higher cost. Does the larger family of reparameterizations capture significantly more games?
Connection to known tractable classes. The classical results on tractable QCQPs — the S-lemma for one quadratic constraint, Burer & Ye’s “at most one negative eigenvalue” condition — work via Lagrange multipliers that convexify the dual. The reparameterization works on the primal. Do the two tractable classes overlap, or is one a strict generalization of the other?
Branch-and-bound on sub-simplices. For games outside the certifiable class, the simplex can be subdivided. On sub-simplices, the gradient \nabla V has a more consistent sign, and (SDP) becomes easier. Can systematic subdivision give a practical exact algorithm even for hard instances?

title: Finding the Best Equilibrium in Potential Games
text:
References

Monderer, D. & Shapley, L.S. (1996). Potential games. Games and Economic Behavior.
Sandholm, W.H. (2010). Population Games and Evolutionary Dynamics. MIT Press.
Conitzer, V. & Sandholm, T. (2003). Complexity results about Nash equilibria. IJCAI.
Bomze, I.M. (1998). On standard quadratic optimization problems. J. Global Optim.
Burer, S. & Ye, Y. (2020). Exactness of Parrilo’s conic approximations. Math. Oper. Res.
Yakubovich, V.A. (1971). The S-procedure in nonlinear control theory. Vestnik Leningrad Univ.
